responseSet.title: The Colonial Inheritance in Theory and Practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Colonial Inheritance in Theory and Practice11

Fiona Gardner

Fiona Gardner

11 Chaucer Road, Bath, BA2 4QU.

Fiona Gardner  

This paper explores the psychological effects of colonisation and the power relationship that develops between coloniser and colonised. External and internal aspects of the idea of the colonisation of the personality and the structure of racism are described, and the inter-psychic and intra-psychic relationship between coloniser and colonised explored. It is suggested that feelings of humiliation, shame and guilt are one aspect of the colonial relationship. These themes are illustrated by extracts from psychotherapy with two adolescents sent to this country from Hong Kong. The psychotherapy took place during the time of the transfer of power from Britain to China. Transference and countertransference issues are discussed, and questions raised about the relative influence of personal and cultural concerns in the therapeutic relationship. Tentative conclusions are drawn.

An Integrative Approach to Severe Emotional Disorders

Giovanni Zapparoli and Maria Gislon

Maria Gislon

Via Vincenzo Bellini 10, 20122 Milan.

Maria Gislon  

This paper presents a model of integrative treatment for serious mental disorders based on the identification of specific needs and characteristics of the patient by means of a psychoanalytic approach. The treatment includes different types of bio-psycho-social techniques, both therapeutic and rehabilitative, which are utilised according to specific selection criteria. The authors describe the most important features of the conceptual framework and illustrate its clinical application through a case-study example.

responseSet.title: Understanding Violent Behaviour within the Boundaries of a Locked Ward
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Violent Behaviour within the Boundaries of a Locked Ward

Siobhán O'Connor

Siobhán O'Connor

16 Newtownbreda Road Belfast BT8 6AS.

Siobhán O'Connor  

In the management of patients who are violent or antisocial in their behaviour, attention to safety is a priority. Physical interventions which are required to provide safety have the effect of altering the dynamics between patients and nurses, with forces towards regression in both. The nurses have to stay alert to potential danger so that they can intervene quickly when necessary. This can alter their responses to patients towards a parental approach, one which has been recognised as a problem in institutions. In regression, the patients have the opportunity for affectionate relationships otherwise denied. Unless a space for reflection is available, many patients who could otherwise benefit from psychological intervention are trapped in regression. This space is provided by the work of the multidisciplinary team. An interpretative intervention is described, one in which the patients were alerted to a potential external reality of a concerned object. The patients’ responses showed a capacity for psychological mindedness which had been obscured by behaviour. In a setting with limited resources, a psychoanalytically informed approach proved to have major therapeutic impact.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

In this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy we start with a paper by Siobhàn O'Connor, a previous contributor to the journal. She is a psychoanalyst who, unusually and importantly, works as an NHS consultant in general psychiatry on a ward for severely disturbed and violent patients. Her theoretical model informs her work in important ways and enables her to reflect on the complex ward dynamics of which she is a part. She has interesting things to say about the inevitably regressive aspects of a situation where patients have at times to be forcibly restrained by nurses. She discusses the complementary ‘parental’ roles, both assumed and enacted by nursing and medical staff on such a ward, and shows how psychotherapeutic interventions can still be made in this extreme setting.

Giovanni Zapparoli and Maria Gislon, also working with patients with severe disorder who are treatment-resistant, contribute two linked papers. In an account of an integrated, multidisciplinary model used by their innovative clinical research team in Italy, they describe an approach to assessment and treatment of such patients which is broadly informed by psychoanalytic principles, but which takes into account biological and social factors. Their particular emphasis is on the assessment of needs as subjectively perceived by the patient. They point out ways in which these may differ radically from the goals and needs often assumed by professionals on the patient's behalf, leading to apparently inexplicable resistance to treatment.

Gary Winship, also a past contributor, presents some interesting ideas about a torturous cycle of destruction and repair in drug addicts which he links to the myth of Prometheus. Vivid clinical vignettes are presented from his own NHS drug addiction service.

Stern's paper emerges from his work as a medical psychoanalytic psychotherapist consulting to a specialist hospital for diseases of the lower gut. His rich clinical material illustrates vividly the severe psychopathology he encounters in his work, and the significant contributions that can be made by such a clinician.

[[RunningHead]]

Family Support Work at the Anna Freud Centre**

Mary Donovan

Mary Donovan

21 Marsfield Gardens, London NW3 5SD.

Mary Donovan  

This paper outlines the context for the emergence and development of a family support service at the Anna Freud Centre. Its main aim is that of helping us think about the therapeutic space to which family support work belongs. Drawing on a range of case material, it describes a flexible and somewhat open-ended model of practice that is emerging in our work and which seems well suited to this particular area of practice. Inter-agency communication and service integration are also seen as integral to this work, and the paper argues that, in this way, professionals can mirror to families in difficulty a holding capacity which has been damaged or disrupted for the family, and which impedes their capacity to address their difficulties.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

It is a great pleasure to write the introduction to this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, my first since taking over as Editor from Jane Milton. This Journal occupies a unique place in our professional concerns, focusing as it does, not simply on the theory, process, and technique of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, but on all of that, when psychotherapy is provided within the publicly-funded setting.

Psychoanalysis has always acknowledged the crucial importance of the setting, understanding it to be a fundamental aspect of the treatment itself. It seems vital, therefore, to have a forum for the development of our understanding of treatments derived from psychoanalysis, but within these very special conditions.

These are challenging times for the Journal: we face competition with a number of training organisations now producing journals of their own. Michael Sinason, the Journal's first editor, has been busy developing the APP website, on which the Journal will be well represented. However, nothing is as effective as personal recommendation, and we are reliant on our readers to help promote what we do.

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy within the NHS is constantly threatened; the Journal provides a forum not for just writing about what we do, but also for sharing our experience of trying to protect it. At the same time, there has been an increase in the numbers of organisations within the voluntary sector, offering treatment based more or less unambiguously on psychoanalysis. More training organisations are seeking BCP accreditation, and some of their members find work in the public sphere. It is extremely important these settings and individuals feel supported and represented by the Journal and by the APP. I should like to ask Journal readers to ensure that colleagues in the voluntary sector and in the wider health and social services know about us. Some back and current issues of the Journal are available, if you know of colleagues or organisations we might tempt to subscribe. We would like to ask all readers who belong to an institutional library which does not currently subscribe, to fill in the enclosed library recommendation form. Our need and wish to increase the circulation of the Journal is not primarily about making money, but about ensuring we continue to have an influence and encouraging contributions from as wide a range of colleagues as possible.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Mind of One's Own: a Kleinian view of self and object. By Robert Caper. Routledge New Library of Psychoanalysis, 32. £17.99. Pp. 165.

Pip Garvey

Pip Garvey

Pip Garvey  

Enormously influenced by Bion, Caper draws on Freud, Klein, Strachey and contemporary analysts to follow the development of a number of crucial interrelated theoretical concepts. Caper writes with clarity and lightness of touch, synthesising ideas and untangling confusions in a delightfully direct style free from fluff or wordiness. Points are made with impact and ideas given zing by being described from unexpected angles, or with a reversal of perspective. For example, Caper on transference:

the relationships that the patient seems to have had with his objects in the past are a ‘repetition’ of the patient's present relationship with the analyst.

I found Caper's writing on theory full of memorable images and unexpectedly more vivid and alive than his patients’ material.

The book is a compilation of a number of separate articles, most of which have been published before, between 1994 and 1998. Despite some ideas being repeated, the articles work well as a book, as the chapters explore the processes of introjection and projection in increasing depth.

Caper contrasts psychoanalysis with suggestion (early psychoanalysis and many current psychotherapies). Without mincing words, he describes the suggestionist as someone actively working to seduce the patient away from facing the truth: encouraging splitting, discouraging thought, evading rather than confronting problems, and

constrained by the strait-jacket into which he has allowed himself to be tied by his own and the patient's narcissism.

Not that psychoanalysts are free from this sort of behaviour but, as Caper emphasises, the point in analysis is to explore the forces that support suggestion. Much of the book is an investigation into what Caper refers to as

the mind-numbing pull that operates on the analyst to enjoy the intimacy and sense of power of a narcissistic merger instead of treating the patient's state of mind as an object of knowledge”.

If he is to resist infection from the patient, the analyst needs a mind of his own with a relationship to his own internal objects, which crucially include a longing for the kind of knowledge psychoanalysis brings and an awareness of its limitations.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Models of the Mind: An Introduction By Joseph Sandler, Alex Holder, Christopher Dare and Anna Ursula Dreher. Karnac. Pp. xvi+203. £16.95.

Marie Bridge

Marie Bridge

Marie Bridge  

This excellent book is aimed at teachers of Freud and their students. It is essentially a re-working of the well-known papers by the first three authors on ‘Frames of reference in psychoanalytic psychology’, published in the 1970s in the British Journal of Medical Psychology.

Traditionally, students of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy begin their training with a study of the major Freud texts. As these authors acknowledge,

…formal training in psychoanalysis has a dual aim — to train people to be scholars and to be clinicians.

An implicit third aim, which also informs the spirit of this book, is that the student should identify with Freud's struggle, and the doubts that eventually force a new paradigm into being. As with infant-observation, staying with the complexity and ambiguity in Freud, while at the same time tracing developmental lines, becomes one of the foundations of a psychoanalytical identity.

As we all know, the student is faced with the difficulties of Freud right at the outset, when she is least experienced clinically and least familiar with the ideas that are to become her conceptual tools. There is the added complication that some of Freud's earliest theoretical writing is the least accessible to modern clinicians, and yet some of the basic concepts are there from the start (drive, defence, primary and secondary process, psychological determinism — indeed, the existence of the unconscious itself).

87

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

John Bowlby: His Early Life. A Biographical Journey into the Roots of Attachment Theory. By Suzan Van Dijken. Free Association Books, Pp. vi+214.

Anne Ward

Anne Ward

Anne Ward  

The author is a researcher in the Centre for Child and Family Studies at Rijks University in Leiden. She chose to write about Bowlby's life up till 1951, the year in which his WHO report, Maternal Care and Mental Health, was published, and Bowlby himself was 44. The report propelled him into the public eye, and also marked a theoretical turning-point in his thinking as he searched for a theoretical framework in which to explain his findings. The resulting ‘attachment theory’ would bring both controversy and further fame. The earlier part of Bowlby's development is much less well documented, but likely to be of particular interest, given his later preoccupation with attachment experiences in childhood.

We are offered an ‘intellectual’ biographical study.

The choice of an intellectual biographical study and not another type of biographical study is made because only by paying attention to Bowlby's intellectual development will light be thrown upon possible roots of separation and the ways in which Bowlby thought about separation.

Van Dijken has achieved this in a fascinating, thorough, and readable form. Bowlby's journey is presented chronologically. Each chapter is introduced and summarised, so that one can collect from time to time the vast array of detail provided. As befits a Bowlbian, Van Dijken attests to the importance of transgenerational influences, and begins with his grandparents. We learn of a particularly traumatic loss for Bowlby's father at the age of 5, when his own father was murdered and subsequently buried in China. Bowlby's own father was often unavailable, either seeing private patients in the home, or building up a successful career as a hospital surgeon. His mother ‘saw’ the children at each end of the day, but otherwise they were largely entrusted to the care of nannies and nursemaids. This was not an unusual pattern in well-off families, but there is little sense of any special bond between Bowlby and his mother. He himself thought he had been ‘sufficiently hurt but not sufficiently damaged’ by his childhood experiences.

[[RunningHead]]

Dynamic Psychotherapy Explained. By Patricia Hughes. Abingdon: Radliffe Medical Press. Pp. 162. £16.95.

Siobhan Murphy

Siobhan Murphy

Siobhan Murphy  

Patricia Hughes describes how, as a medical student and junior psychiatrist interested in psychotherapy, she was unable to find a text which was pitched at the right level. In this beautifully clear book, she brings together her wide experience of the provision and teaching of dynamic psychotherapy to produce a text aimed specifically at undergraduate medical and nursing students and beginners in psychiatry across all disciplines. It is not only the content, but also the style of this book which is different from the commonly used introductory texts of psychotherapy. As it is designed to allow trainees with limited time to access the fundamental aspects of dynamic psychotherapy and its provision within the NHS, each chapter begins with a highlighted box containing the areas to be covered. As an example, Chapter One, A Developmental Model of the Mind, begins with a box containing the following:

Brain and mind. The mind-brain relationship. Born or made: the nature-nurture debate. The neurobiology of emotion. The effect of experience on the developing brain. The effect of severe deprivation on the developing brain. The effect of experience on the mature brain. The nature of mental representations. Mental representations and relating to the world.

This style is a very effective taster of the menu to come, and entices the reader into each chapter which can either be treated as a selection of tapas-like dishes or enjoyed as a complete meal. The ease of dipping in and out of each chapter is heightened by bulletin boxes which summarise the main points of each area. For example, the section on brain and mind in Chapter One begins with a highlighted box containing the five main points which are elaborated on in the text. This teaching method has been crudely described as:

say what you're going to talk about; talk about it; say what you've talked about.

It is, however, based on sound educational theories, whose effective application to teaching psychotherapy has previously been elegantly described by Hughes & Gibson (1996).

[[RunningHead]]

Facing Reality: Work with a 4-Year-Old and a 34-Year-Old11

Paul Barrows

Paul Barrows

120 West Heath Road, London NW3 7TU.

Paul Barrows  

The author takes as his starting point the view that mental health requires the existence of a satisfactory relationship between internal (psychic) and external reality. He discusses the nature of the ‘membrane’ that exists between these two realities and the qualities required for it to function adequately. He explores the possible developmental origins of this ‘membrane’, and the different ways in which its establishment may miscarry. He illustrates one of the ways in which this process may go wrong when the ‘membrane’ has become excessively reinforced. He illustrates this with clinical material from a schizoid, adult patient who had taken refuge in external reality and with material from a child patient with autistic spectrum disorder. Links are made to Freud's views on the establishment of the Reality Principle, the process of splitting and the importance of the mechanism of disavowal. There is some speculation as to the possible aetiology of the pathology in the two cases described, and the developmental importance of acquiring a valid sense of potency.

responseSet.title: From the Efficacy to the Effectiveness Model in Psychotherapy Research: The APP Multi-Centre Project
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Efficacy to the Effectiveness Model in Psychotherapy Research: The APP Multi-Centre Project

Marco Chiesa and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Richmond TW10 7JF
(e-mail: marco@cassel.ftech.co.uk)

London WC1E 6BT
(e-mail: p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk)

Peter Fonagy  

In this paper we discuss the differences that exists between the efficacy and the effectiveness models in psychotherapy research. We argue that randomized-controlled trials have several shortcomings and that they may not be the most appropriate method for evaluating psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Well-designed field studies may provide an informative, relevant and complementary approach to the evaluation of the effectiveness of psychotherapy as delivered in routine clinical practice. An example of a multi-centre effectiveness study is presented.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What is the Role of Pre-Assessment Questionnaires in Psychotherapy?

Andrew Soutter and Antony Garelick

Antony Garelick

714 Forest Road, London E17 3HP.

Antony Garelick  

The value, of using a pre-assessment questionnaire on patients referred to a NHS clinic for psychotherapy is explored. Two groups of patients, one of which was requested to complete a questionnaire, were studied. Rates of non-attendance, suitability for psychotherapy, and subsequent entry into psychotherapy, were compared. The effectiveness and multifaceted nature of such a questionnaire, together with its function from an institutional perspective, is explored in the light of these findings.

The results suggest that use of a questionnaire enhances the efficiency of the assessment process, but at the cost of being an added barrier, which prevents some patients from attending and thus obtaining psychotherapy. Psychoanalytic psychotherapy in the NHS is presented with all kinds of pressures, including the pressure to remain true to its analytical aim, to have closer links with psychiatry, to be scientific with proven effectiveness, as well as to have a different kind of institutional effectiveness which relates more to turnover of patients. The function of the psychotherapy questionnaire in this context is discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Supervision of Individual Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in Institutions! The Setting, the Dynamics and the Learning Process11

Dimitris Anastasopoulos and John Tsiantis

John Tsiantis

6 Spetson Str. 15451 N. Psychico, Athens.

11527 Goudi, Athens.

John Tsiantis  

The supervision of psychotherapy in institutions can take a variety of forms. In all cases, the dynamics of the triad that consists of the patient, the therapist and the supervisor is interwoven with the dynamics of the institution itself, of the staff team and of the relation between the supervisor and the service. As a consequence, the supervision is influenced by many more uncontrolled variables than usual. The supervisor has to take into consideration, and to balance, the real facts about the setting and the needs of trainees, as well as the dynamics of the team and the organisation, which produce massive projections and multiple transference, which inevitably triggers feelings of countertransference. The supervision process is affected not only by the dynamics of the supervisor -supervisee-patient triad, but by the dynamics of the institution which provides the supervisory setting.

responseSet.title: Addiction, Death, and the Liver in Mind: The Prometheus Syndrome
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Addiction, Death, and the Liver in Mind: The Prometheus Syndrome

Gary Winship

Gary Winship

53-55 Argyle Road, Reading, RG1 7YL.

Gary Winship  

Using illustrative clinical material, drug-addiction is shown to be characterised by repeated sadistic attacks on internal objects perceived by the addict to be dead or dying. Damaged internal objects appear resuscitated in order that they might be repaired, though the result is a continuation of a self-destructive attack. This compulsive cycle of repetitive attack and attempt at repair is compared with the punishment cycle in the Greek myth of Prometheus. Particular attention is paid to the attack on Prometheus's liver. It is proposed that the process of repairing the liver might be considered in terms of a need for detoxification of noxious internal objects. It is proposed that the toxic mentation of addiction might be thought of as a specific object-relations syndrome.

responseSet.title: Facing the Crisis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Facing the Crisis

Isabel Menzies-Lyth

Isabel Menzies-Lyth

Townsend Close, 50 Church Way, Iffley, Oxford, OX4 4EF.

Isabel Menzies-Lyth  

The crisis in Nursing is multi-faceted, though the facets are dynamically and intimately connected. Central to the whole problem is the crisis in patient care. The increase in serious complaints about nursing care is only the ‘tip of the iceberg’. Smaller failures are rife, and cause patients a great deal of suffering and distress as well as possibly slowing their recovery and prolonging their stay in hospital. Failures in patient care also distress nurses and are a not-insignificant factor in a more public aspect of the crisis: the heavy loss of experienced nurses from the hospital service and the difficulty in recruiting and retaining good students. In the end, the responsibility for the quality of nursing care lies with nurse-managers.1 It follows that if nursing care is poor one should look to how managers are functioning. It seems that ward-management has deteriorated since the ward sister, who was not a ‘manager’ — just ‘ran’ her ward — provided better patient care.

I gave a paper forty years ago to a group of Health Service professionals who were heads of units. I began by saying “I am going to address you today as managers”. That opening evoked a wave of outrage. The audience thought of themselves as caring professionals and dissociated themselves from managers who, in their fantasies, were ruthless, inhuman and authoritarian. Such a remark would not provoke outrage today. Being a manager is commonly explicit, and consciously accepted. But the dislike of being a manager has not gone. It is expressed more subtly and insidiously, but nonetheless effectively. It seriously interferes with managers committing themselves to their task, and performing well.

Subtle socially-structured defences seem to have grown up that contribute to this situation (Menzies-Lyth 1988). There is confusion and vagueness about the role and functions of a ward manager. I have tried many times to elicit some clear description, and have singularly failed. I think this is deliberate, if unconscious. To face up fully to what being a ward manager means provokes too much anxiety. Consequently, ward-management is to some extent based on misconceptions about what is required: to provide a total system of nursing care based on a good understanding of what patients need.

[[RunningHead]]

Midwifery of the Soul: A Holistic Perspective on Psychoanalysis. By Margaret Arden. Free Association Books. Pp 126. £35 (hb), £15.95 (pb).

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner  

The main title of this book draws our attention, but the subtitle tells us what it is about. Margaret Arden is a long-experienced British ‘Independent’ psychoanalyst who is affectionately relied upon to say, quietly and clearly, exactly what she thinks. These papers show her incisive logical mind at work — at ease with psychoanalysis and its history, she writes with clear simplicity.

She is a no-nonsense psychoanalyst, but is also interested in recognising that events, physical and mental, need, among other things, to be understood by their place in the patterns of the whole systems to which they belong. Simple one-to-one cause-and-effect explanation is good enough for everyday life; and it dominated the nineteenth-century physics and biology in which Freud grew up. However, Einstein began to break this hold; and by the 1950s biological, psychological and social sciences had grasped the importance of systems thinking. This was, I think, a historic revolution. Arden herself refers to such a systems approach as ‘holistic’. For some, this word might indicate a romantic dottiness, but there is none of this in her thinking.

In fact she turns her mind to wondering about the nature of whole systems, on both microscopic and cosmic scales. For instance, we have a crucial explanation of how identical structures can shape themselves quite differently according to where they are placed in a whole system. Thus identical muscle cells shape themselves differently according to where they are in a muscle system. The same applies psychologically to people — they change according to where they belong.

Arden shows a gift for quiet and careful wondering about whole systems and their parts. This often becomes a fascinating wonderment, even with religious glimpses at times. It will be strange to many workaday therapists, but she holds her quiet precision and scepticism, so that even convinced atheists will find it hard to dismiss her.

The first chapter is about two systemic thinkers of the later twentieth century who had greatness. These are: Gregory Bateson, sociologist and investigator of family communications; and Ignacio Matte Blanco, psychoanalyst and investigator of the logic of the individual's emotionality and unconscious. The modes of thought of these men were very different, but both used formal logic creatively. Arden does not use formal logic but she arouses in us eager thoughtfulness about it. For instance, of Matte Blanco she says “[He] produces an indescribable feeling of discovering the truth that one has always known but been unable to express [p. 14]”.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A History of Child Psychoanalysis. By Claudine & Pierre Geissmann. New Library of Psychoanalysis No. 30. Routledge.

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen  

A History of Child Psychoanalysis is a scholarly work on the subject. The book is divided into four sections:

Part I The Day Before Yesterday: Beginnings in Vienna (1905-20)

Part II Yesterday: two schools, two cities — Vienna, Berlin, London (1920-45)

Part III Today: Spread of child psychoanalysis throughout the world from 1945

Part IV And tomorrow?

The translation from the French, closely overseen by the Editor, Elizabeth Spillius, is satisfactory. However, the chapters do not easily ‘flow’. This may be due to the authors’ copious use of documents and letters to illustrate their points, which at times may detract from the overall focus of their exposition.

The authors have taken great care to give a balanced and well-informed chronological view of the relevant scientific developments. These are understood within the social and cultural context of the period. References to the personal histories of the psychoanalysts involved are brought in only to delineate and highlight their scientific contributions. The authors’ balanced approach is clearly the strength of the book. However, I suggest that the attempt to be fair and all-inclusive may also be a limitation of the book. Important points and arguments, such as the contributions of Melanie Klein and Anna Freud, are woven throughout several chapters without necessarily a clear view or conclusion from the authors. This apparent lack of clear ‘pointers’ may make the reader's experience less fruitful than intended.

An attractive feature is the inclusion of chapters on child analysis outside Britain, including North and South America and France. I found the chapters on the early French pioneers Eugénie Sokolnicka and Sophie Morgenstern very interesting. I anticipate the chapters on developments in Argentina may also attract the attention of Anglo-Saxon readers, unfamiliar with the history of child analysis in distant lands.

I found it useful to learn about the history of the development of ‘Child Guidance Centre’ and community child mental health teams in France in the 1950s and ‘60s, and to compare it with developments in England. Child analysts were instrumental in creating psychoanalytic services and clinics in France after the war, such as the CMPPs (Centres-Médico-Psycho-Pédagogiques) in France, and the Institut Claparède. The development of the CMPPs was affected from the beginning by “a tension between the desire to create a psychoanalytic consulting centre and the resistance of the authorities” (p. 300). Claudine Geissmann started her own CMPP in 1965.

[[RunningHead]]

Michael Balint: Object Relations Pure and Applied. By Harold Stewart, with chapters by Andrew Elder and Robert Gosling. Routledge. £13.99. Pp. 146.

Debbie Bellman

Debbie Bellman

Debbie Bellman  

In this book, Balint's contributions to psychoanalytic theory and applied psychoanalysis are outlined, examined, and put into an historical context in a way that is admirable for its clarity and condensation. While Balint was one of a group of psychoanalysts who “developed and advanced what is known as the British object relations school of psychoanalysis” (Preface), and while many of his views have been incorporated into psychoanalytic thinking, they have perhaps not received the recognition they deserve. If not entirely overtly stated, it seems unquestionable that a main aim of the book is to try to reestablish Balint as a prominent psychoanalytic thinker.

The first, and main, portion of the book is devoted to Balint's theories on psychoanalysis; the second comprising chapters by Andrew Elder and Robert Gosling, to Balint's contributions to applied work in the realm of general practice. In the first section, Balint's published work is discussed chronologically, enabling the reader to get a sense of the development of his ideas. At the same time, there are a number of ideas that Balint did not develop fully, notably his view that there are separate lines of development for instinctual drives and object relations, and his theories do not form an integrated whole. Stewart suggests that these factors may contribute to Balint's receiving insufficient recognition, as may the relative lack of clinical examples to support and explicate his ideas on theory and technique. It is nevertheless clear that Balint was an enormously creative, courageous, and pioneering thinker and practitioner. My experience in reading the book, as one who has only dabbled in his work, was both of learning something new and of recognising Balint as the source, or one of the sources, of ideas previously known.

Balint was, for example, among the first group of analysts, following Ferenczi, firmly committed to the concept of the primacy of object relations, beginning with the infant's primary state of relatedness to the environment.

Shadow of the Other — Intersubjectivity and Gender in Psychoanalysis. By Jessica Benjamin. New York and London: Routledge. Pp. 129. £12.99.

Emilia Steuerman

Emilia Steuerman

Emilia Steuerman  

Jessica Benjamin is a psychoanalyst, a feminist, and a philosopher working in the United States. She is particularly interested in the relationship between the self and the other, and how the identifications with (or negations of) the other are at the core of our identities. Indeed, the question of subjectivity and the world (or self and the other) is a crucial and overlapping area of study for philosophy, feminism and psychoanalysis. The book is divided into three parts, all of them related to the main theme of the recognition and denial of the self and the other in the more general quest for ‘authorship’ as a true sense of self.

Resistance to Change in the Psychotic Patient

Giovanni Zapparoli and Maria Gislon

Maria Gislon

Via Vincenzo Bellini 10, 20122 Milan.

Maria Gislon  

This paper makes a contribution to the understanding of some different forms of resistance to change found in severe mental disorders, whose treatment is often marked by exhausting phases of ‘illusion’ and ‘disappointment’. A conceptual framework is provided, both for the evaluation of the problem and for a multiple approach to treatment based on an integrative conceptual model, and is illustrated through the presentation and discussion of case-study examples.

Hope: A Shield in the Economy of Borderline States. By Anna Potamianou. Translated by Philip Slotkin New Library of Psychoanalysis 26. Routledge. Pp. vii + 120. £45 (h/b) £14 (p/b).

Matthew Patrick

Matthew Patrick

Matthew Patrick  

At the centre of this book is an interesting and challenging idea: that hope is not always a good thing. In particular, the author sets out a convincing argument for the role that hope may play in the economy and maintenance of certain aspects of borderline psychopathology.

Potamianou contends that central to the pathology of borderline states are difficulties in the regulation of internal and external boundaries. In relation to this she sees two economic aspects of mental functioning as being of special importance: difficulties in maintaining the balance between cathexes and countercathexes; and the low degree of fusion of the life and death instincts. In the economy of non-borderline individuals Potamianou identifies hope as serving the function of a binding cathexis,

based on a stable image of the subject's self-esteem, which accepts the idea of the good that is to come, and trusts in future possibilities and the relation to objects.

This is the type of hope that we are all, I think, used to thinking about. This is hope that derives from the individual's capacity to establish and preserve a good internal object.

This is the second book in this remarkable series, remarkable in its bringing together of views and practice from all over Europe a tribute to the European Federation for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy and personally to Brian Martindale for his unfailing energy in founding the EFPP and then furthering its work. The first title is Countertransference in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy with Children and Adolescents.

responseSet.title: The Crisis in Nursing: Reflections on the Crisis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Crisis in Nursing11

Reflections on the Crisis

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius 39 Willes Road, London, NW5 3DN

Julia Fabricius  

I started training as a nurse exactly thirty years ago, and my mother started her training thirty years before that, in 1939. Inevitably, my reflections in 1999 are situated within that historical context, a view from a bridge under which has passed not only my own experience, but the handed-down narratives received from my mother. Of course reflection is always a personal matter and my reflections today will be idiosyncratic, and perhaps provocative; but I hope that, by all of us loosening too tight a control on our thoughts and associations, we may be able to have some creative interchange.

For me the word ‘crisis’ immediately conjures up my mother's accounts of nursing patients with pneumonia before antibiotics were available. My mother explained to me that the crisis was reached at the point when the temperature was at its height and the patient at his most sick. At the crisis, either the fever broke and the patient started to recover, or he continued to deteriorate and died. Could it be that this association of mine is apposite to the condition of today's sick patient, the Nursing profession itself? I do believe that Nursing is, to say the least, extremely unwell. I think that my analogy, of the crisis in pre-antibiotic pneumonia, is useful because the patient was very ill indeed and the two crucial elements in his recovery were his own natural resistance and the skill of his nurses. I shall say more about both of these.

What is this crisis in Nursing about? It can of course be analysed in many ways: politically, sociologically; in terms of pay, working hours, changes in the Health Service, changes in public expectation. Nurses feel badly used and held in low esteem. In this brief presentation I am going to suggest that the crisis is primarily due to the sickness in the Nursing profession, and that this sickness, like many psychological illnesses, is caused by a deficit in professional self-esteem. Of course, no one would disagree that self-esteem in the profession is low at present, and that it is part of the low morale. But my suggestion is that the low self-esteem is the cause rather than the effect of the crisis. I imagine this will be a controversial view; and, of course, like any explanation of a complex situation, it can in reality only be a partial explanation.

responseSet.title: ‘Numbers and Faces’ Economic Analyses and Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Numbers and Faces’ Economic Analyses and Psychotherapy

Eileen McGinley

Eileen McGinley

London, SE5 8AZ.

Eileen McGinley  

This paper addresses the difficulties which surround the economic analysis of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Economic evaluations have as their explicit aim the allocation of scarce resources. The methodology of economic research remains crude, with little consensus over what constitutes costs and benefits, and how they can be measured in the field of mental health. The value of psychotherapy may be undermined by narrow economic considerations, for both defensive and destructive purposes, and we need to differentiate between these differing attitudes. Using one of Auden's poems, ‘Numbers and Faces’, the paper addresses how economic analysis takes a perspective on psychotherapy that focuses more on ‘numbers’, as opposed to process research that more sticks to ‘faces’. The detrimental effects of adopting a belief in numbers is discussed. Two clinical vignettes highlight the limited application of economic evaluation to the clinical setting.

[[RunningHead]]

Siblings in the Unconscious and Psychopathology. By Vamik D. Volkan & Gabriel Ast. Madison: International Universities Press. Pp. 184.

Victoria Davenport

Victoria Davenport

Victoria Davenport  

While there is a mass of material on childhood sibling experience, the entire area of internalised sibling experience in adults has remained largely unexplored. Why? One reason may be that Freud himself had unresolved, conflicting feelings about the death of his brother Julius, when Sigmund was seventeen months old. And indeed, for many of us, there may be unconscious, unresolved, feelings towards siblings, making this an uncomfortable area at which to look too closely. The idea of a sibling transference and countertransference seems plausible and interesting; yet is has never been given much thought.

Volkan & Ast's book is thus a ground-breaker. In the authors’ own words:

In our book, as psychoanalysts of adults, we analyse adults’ internal worlds in order to illustrate the fate of childhood sibling experiences and their pathological influences. We try to answer the underlying question: How does the adult's mental representation of a sibling evolve from that which he or she formed in childhood?

At first glance, the chapter-headings read like B-grade horror films: The Intruder; Insects, Fish, Birds and Animals; the Secret Restaurant; To Kill or to Repair; Dead Sibling Representations; Charon and Herakles; Living with a Dead Brother. However, while the text does not exactly flow, and the need to include fairly basic theory can be irritating at times, the mass of clinical material is thought-provoking and significant.

‘If one piece of the pie goes to the baby, then there will be less for me!’ is the theme in The Intruder, which focuses on the arrival of the new baby and the powerful feelings evoked in older children by the intrusion, and how this manifests itself in later life, leading to extreme competitiveness or passivity.

Internal sibling representation often occurs in symbolic form, and in Insects, Fish, birds and Animals we are given detailed clinical examples of how an animal, for example, can become the object of displaced unacceptable feelings towards a sibling. Here, a 38-year-old single woman is presented who complains of claustrophobia, referring to children as ‘intruders’, and who has two cats that she introduces to the therapist ‘as if they were family members’.

Susan Davison

Susan Davison

Susan Davison  

This book is one of the Tavistock Clinic Series and its contributors are all members of the Unit for the Study of Trauma and its Aftermath in the Adult Department of the Tavistock Clinic. Caroline Garland founded the Unit in 1986, following the dreadful news of the sinking of the Herald of Free Enterprise off the Coast of Belgium. The name of that ferry was eerily prophetic, as a spate of further major accidents in the late 80s brought to the public mind the psychological cost of those caught up in life-threatening events beyond their control. Serious questions began to be raised about the wisdom of deregulation in the pursuit of profit. In the wake of these disasters a number of centres of excellence have set up units for the study and treatment of post-traumatic stress disorder. The Tavistock Trauma Unit is unique in seeking to apply a rigorously psychoanalytic methodology to its research in the belief that, at least for some, this will be therapeutic.

The Trauma Unit offers a consultation service to individuals who have suffered a traumatic event, whether recently or long ago, when the patient identifies the event as the cause of their current psychological distress.

responseSet.title: Paula Heimann's Question and Group Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paula Heimann's Question and Group Analysis

John Gordon

John Gordon

Uxbridge Road, Southall, Middlesex, UB1 3EU.

John Gordon  

Paula Heimann asked a famous question: “Why is the patient now doing what to whom?”, and said “The answer to this question constitutes the transference interpretation”. In the individual setting, sometimes the answer is obvious, but more often it is obscure. Only a complex and painful integration in the mind of the therapist of manifest verbal and non-verbal material, with subtle countertransference feelings and the ‘atmospherics’ of the session, can point to a possible description of the unconscious interna! relationship indicated by Heimann.

When this work takes place in a group setting, it is further complicated by the conscious and unconscious currents intrinsic to group interaction. Furthermore, the many patients now treated in formal group psychotherapy experience severe problems with their identity, ranging from the identity diffusion and rigidities of Personality Disorder to the fragmentation and confusion of more psychotic patients. Such additional factors must be confronted if a response, let alone an answer, can be given to Heimann's question from the group perspective. An account of a group session shows how the author considers Heimann's question in his clinical practice, and, by means of this question, approaches the application of psychoanalytic concepts to groups.

Counselling and Psychotherapy in Primary Health Care: a psychodynamic approach. By Jan Wiener and Mannie Sher. Macmillan. £11.99. Pp. 167.

Jo O'Reilly

Jo O'Reilly

Jo O'Reilly  

There has been a rapid development in counselling and psychotherapy in General Practice. Whilst there is a growing literature on counselling in primary health care, less has been written about psychodynamic psychotherapy in

281

this context. This comprehensive book is written by a Jungian analyst and a psychoanalytical psychotherapist, both of whom have extensive experience of working in General Practice. It is one of the Basic Texts in Counselling and Psychotherapy series, aimed at a wide range of professionals, and as such introduces psychodynamic ideas in an accessible manner, with an informative index. Up to a third of general practices now employ some form of counselling help. Little is known of the range of training and experience represented, and I suspect this is a potential problem for GPs’ patients, and this book attempts to address this. It is known that therapeutic styles range from Rogerian counselling to psychodynamic psychotherapy to ‘eclectic’, with a worrying 28 per cent of counsellors having no formal qualification. In writing about counselling/psychotherapy in primary health care, the authors are therefore describing a broad church; and although some of the opportunities and challenges available in General Practice apply whatever form of counselling/psychotherapy is applied, it is also the case that there are considerable differences in beliefs and techniques within these disciplines.

The book gives a realistic account of the opportunities and challenges of the work. It is striking that in General Practice there is volume and variety of work carried out in an atmosphere of intense need and limited resources. The authors refer to the insatiable and undiscriminating emotional needs of the patients who are making more direct requests for counselling/psychotherapy and they use the metaphor of the ‘souk’ to describe the atmosphere in Primary Care as opposed to the more formal outpatient Psychotherapy Department, the ‘citadel’. I liked this metaphor for capturing the colour, life and variety in the clinical work in General Practice, but was reminded of the wide range of products of varying quality, the presence of fortune-tellers, quacks and fakes, and the need for guidance and a discriminating eye in the intense atmosphere of the market-place.

In this paper, the author describes aspects of his work as a psychoanalytical psychotherapist in a hospital for diseases of the colon and rectum. [[RunningHead]]

Reason and Passion: A Celebration of the work of Hanna Segal. Edited by David Bell. Duckworth. Tavistock Clinic Series. £14.95.

Penelope Garvey

Penelope Garvey

Penelope Garvey  

This is the first of two volumes of collected papers — many of which are previously published — edited by David Bell to celebrate the life and work of Hanna Segal, one of the most influential psychoanalysts of our day. In her foreword, Irma Pick describes Segal's ‘incise clarity of mind’, and refers to her ‘fierce loyalty to the people and ideas that she cares about’. It is for these qualities of reason and passion that Segal commands such enormous affection and respect, and it is these that gave Bell the tide for the book.

In his excellent introduction, Bell brings to life Segal's pragmatic realism and punchy style of delivery. I particularly enjoyed his description of the meeting at the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, in which a speaker had suggested that the Society should go along with a situation in which we would be getting ninety-five per cent of what we wanted, and should not be bloody-minded about the other five per cent. Segal replied by saying that it depended on the five per cent. She went on to describe a patient who had met and wanted to marry a man who was kind, intelligent, shared her cultural interests, and so on. There was only one small problem — he was impotent. ‘If,’ said Segal, ‘the five per cent is the balls of the matter, then leaving it out is self-castration!’ Bell also gives an interesting short biography of Segal's life, and a very useful, detailed, and thorough overview of her work.

Bell describes truthfulness as the hallmark of Segal's work. He quotes the lines of Samuel Johnson used by Segal, Bion and Rosenfeld in their joint obituary of Klein:

Whether to see life as it is will offer us much consolation I know not; but the consolation which is gained from truth, if any there be, is solid and durable; that which may be derived from error must be, like its original fallacious and fugitive.

It is this capacity, of knowing what is really there internally and externally, a capacity which involves facing separation, loss and guilt, that Segal considers essential for the development of symbol-formation and creativity.

Gary Winship

Gary Winship

Gary Winship  

The publication of Bion's War diary marks the centenary of his birth. Commenced when he was 20 years old, they cover his years on the front line in France during the First World War. He was awarded the DSO and Légion d'Honneur for his bravery as a tank-commander. Many of Bion's colleagues have attested to his awesome character and dominant persona, and it would seem hard to separate these traits from the young Bion who, out of simple survival, had to learn to face and stare down the enemy. To have to kill to survive, and to stay strong for junior ranks, were the experiences which led Bion to learn how to sit far behind his face.

Jo O'Reilly  

Even Paranoids Have Enemies explores the relationship between paranoia as a state of mind and persecution as an external reality. The title is a quote from Golda Meir in response to Henry Kissinger during the Sinai peace talks, and sets the context for the subsequent chapters in linking intrapsychic, interpersonal, group and international factors in the development of paranoid and persecutory processes. The book arose from a conference at the Arbours Association in London, and comprises a collection of essays, predominantly written from a psychoanalytic perspective, with contributions from psychiatrists, a businessman, and a playwright, from a range of cultural backgrounds.

The book is divided into three parts. Part 1 concentrates on paranoid and persecutory phenomena at an individual level. It comprises five chapters, the first of which gives a developmental account of paranoia, both as a defence which may, in extreme forms, result in psychosis, but is also part of normal development and an aspect of the normal personality. Chapters on work with victims of torture and with children of Holocaust survivors describe vividly extreme forms of persecution, which exist in external reality. Devastating events in history are linked with the existing object relations of the individual, and emphasise the importance of this in our work as psychotherapists in trying to understand the meanings of a real situation of persecution for the individual concerned. The chapters of this section are fascinating and painful, and powerfully make the case for the importance of linking the internal world with the external world in a range of settings, affecting individuals as diverse as Judge Schreber and Brian Keenan.

Part II widens the arena to look at paranoid and persecutory processes in groups. The first chapter, by Otto Kernberg, is a fascinating and evocative account of the regressive power of groups to activate extreme primitive defences in the individual, with regression to paranoid-schizoid functioning.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

This issue begins with two important contributions on nursing, by Julia Fabricius and Isabel Menzies-Lyth, both psychoanalysts with a longstanding commitment to the profession. These papers arose out of a conference for Nurse Managers which was held at the Tavistock Clinic to address some of the issues surrounding the current crisis in nursing. Increasingly, nurses are finding a voice within the APP and we are hoping to publish a series of papers on the contribution of psychoanalytic thinking to nursing in a variety of contexts.

The next paper, by Paul Barrows, illustrates the continuities in the psychotherapeutic treatment of children and adults, while Joan Hunter gives a vivid account of work with an elderly patient.

There are two non-clinical papers, the first by Andrew Soutter and Antony Garelick on the role and usefulness of pre-assessment questionnaires. The second, by Marco Chiesa and Peter Fonagy, describes a project concerned with the changing paradigm of psychotherapy research.

Such a range of fascinating contributions should surely suggest that the Journal is in a fit and healthy state. In some ways of course it is, with authors continuing to work hard to produce papers of a high standard and of real contemporary relevance. The Book Reviews section continues its outstandingly useful role.

We know that APP members value the Journal highly. However, we face increasing competition from new journals, both for papers and for an established place within institutional libraries from whence we can have real influence and authority. I would like to encourage readers to consider submitting their work to the Journal: the in-flow of papers is what keeps the Journal alive, and the Editorial Board will endeavour to make it a useful experience for authors. In addition, we would welcome feedback from readers about the Journal and suggestions for papers, special issues, and changes in content or format. I think this present issue is a particularly strong and interesting one, and hope it will be widely enjoyed.

[[RunningHead]]

Paralysis of Symbolic Functioning in the Child of a Holocaust Survivor

Mona Wilson and Michael Sinason

Michael Sinason

Address for correspondence: 7 Dollis Hill Lane, London, NW2 6JH.

Michael Sinason  

This paper describes the second attempt to offer a patient psychotherapy after a previous psychotherapeutic treatment failed. The patient's presenting difficulties were of dissatisfaction with life and specific problems in thinking and in understanding the use of metaphor. The patient's father had survived five years in a concentration camp but the significance of this fact had been overlooked in the first attempt at therapy. The literature on the families of survivors highlights how there is often an embargo on talking about life in the camps. The inability of the survivors to process the experiences symbolically can give rise to a defect in the ability to understand symbolism and use metaphor in their children. This can result in unconscious pre-symbolic enactment of aspects of the reality of the concentration camp situation, in the present-day life of the child. However, it was difficult to establish whether the patient's defect in symbolic capacity was due to transmission of the Holocaust trauma or had evolved for reasons more related to other developmental difficulties. These problems of differentiation are illustrated in the clinical material and are further discussed in connection with the conceptual, diagnostic and therapeutic issues involved.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Introduction to Object Relations. By Lavinia Gomez. Free Association Books. Pp 245. £15.95.

Neil Morgan

Neil Morgan

Neil Morgan  

This will be a useful primer on object relations, aimed, as it is, at students of psychotherapy of all theoretical approaches, as well as other interested people. Its best feature is that it is so highly accessible, compared with, say, Meir Perlow's Understanding Mental Objects, in comparison with which it suffers in terms of intellectual rigour. It shares something of the approach of Greenberg & Mitchell's Object Relations in Psychoanalytic Theory, in being ‘synthetic’, trying to help the student by illuminating areas of convergence and divergence, which are usually obscured by the complexities and isolation of various analytic schools. Compared with both these books, it is a much easier read, and it would serve as a helpful introduction to either of them.

The idea stressed throughout is the conviction that the self should be viewed as made up of internal relationships, at both conscious and unconscious levels. This immediately places relating, rather than the satisfaction of biological drives, at the centre of psychotherapy. However, Gomez is aware, not least from her position as a practising therapist, that the drive theory will not quite go away. She returns to this theme at several places in the book, trying to find a way to fit these two apparently competing perspectives together, under the realisation that human beings have a foot in both camps: “We seem to live in a psycho-physiological world,” she notes.

The book is divided into two parts: the first devoted to theory; and the second, more ambitiously, to a number of areas under the general heading of applications. In my view, the first section is the better. It consists of seven chapters devoted to the historical development of object relations theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mothering and Ambivalence. Edited by Wendy Holloway & Brid Featherstone. Routledge. Pp 232. £45 (hb), £15.99 (pb).

Kate Pugh

Kate Pugh

Kate Pugh  

“Love means the totality of infant and child care, that which facilitates maturational processes. It includes hate”

— D.W. Winnicott (1963)

Mothering and Ambivalence is a thought-provoking look at current thinking in psychotherapy on mothering, and indeed fathering, putting the discussion in the context of the psychodynamics of ambivalence.

What exactly is meant by ambivalence? And can an understanding of this ‘complex and contradictory state of mind’ (Parker, chap. 2, p. 17) help those of us working with families in trouble? The psychoanalytically-informed thinking in this book, particularly when accompanied by detailed clinical descriptions, make it a useful read for many, including therapists working with parents and their children, and social workers and psychiatrists who make decisions based on a mother's capacity to care for her children.

This is a fascinating and important field of work, and reading this book is like looking down a microscope, starting far away and then focusing down on different aspects of the field in question. The beginning is a ‘bird's-eye view’ of the theoretical and political background to psychotherapy and mothering, which may leave the clinician wanting more substance and clarity. This arrives in the form of an honest and informative description of a counselling service for disturbed women with dependent children (Mayne & Best, chap. 8) which anyone working in this area will identify with and learn from. Further focusing down leads into the inner world of an 8-year-old girl in psychotherapy which engagingly illustrates the concepts involved in ‘maternal ambivalence’ and offers a hopeful picture of its potential resolution (Owens, chap. 9).

Rozsika Parker argues that the mother who can know her ambivalent feelings towards her child can make creative reparation in relation to her baby. She makes the point that motherhood is an important time for developmental change in the mother, not just in the infant and child. It is the dynamic of this reciprocal growth that depends on the acknowledgement of ambivalence which is part-and-parcel of a relationship with another who is separate and yet, in the case of the baby, quite dependent on the mother and/or mother substitutes for survival and growth. Parker refers to Winnicott's paper, ‘Hate in the countertransference’, in which he lists the many reasons that the mother inevitably hates her baby, not least because the baby is not she, is not just her creation and is not under her control, and yet demands her care to survive.

Panic Attacks Late in Life and Change before Life Ends

Joan Hunter

Joan Hunter

15 Elaine Court, 123 Haverstock Hill, London NW3 4RT.

Joan Hunter  

This paper is concerned with two years’ work with a woman who initially was suffering from panic attacks and who was 82 when we started. There is an introduction making reference to some work being carried out with patients suffering from such attacks. The complaints and symptoms of my patient are then described, followed by a personal history incorporating the onset of these. The treatment which is described was unavoidably unorthodox, taking place in the patient's own home and combining group and individual approaches. An account is given of changes in her thinking, arising out of her treatment, and culminating in her thoughts and fears as her life drew to an end.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inside Lives: Psychoanalysis and the Growth of the Personality. By Margot Waddell. Tavistock Clinic Series. Duckworth. Pp. 225. £14.95 (pb).

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn  

Margot Waddell's Inside Lives is clear and direct. It ‘talks to you’ about the

… ordinary ways in which a person may grow up — internally as well as externally.

(p. 95).

It is focused on the development, loss and retrieval of a capacity for thinking, in Bion's sense, and how an individual's ‘predominant identificatory modes’ recur at different stages in life, reactivating the earliest defences against psychic pain. It has substantial insightful chapters on states of mind, models of learning, and stages of development from infancy to old age. You can believe the tributes inside the cover of this very readable volume, such as Mike Brearley's comment that this is a book for ‘specialists and non-specialists alike’, for new perceptions and old truths are conveyed again and again in fresh language supported by accessible case-material from psychoanalysis and psychotherapy.

The book's thesis is developed consistently throughout, to show how psychoanalysis, following Klein's central theories, leads to the illumination of the individual's internal world; and, following Bion's extension of Klein's theory of projective identification, leads us to view containment as therapeutically central. There is a purposive shift away from Freud's emphasis on ‘the quantitative intensity of biologically driven forces’ to an emphasis on the ‘richness of emotional relationships’ (p. xii). A drawback, to my mind, is the insufficient weight given to the body and the power of instinctive forces in severe disturbance, which would get more mention in a Freudian account, and to cruel and perverse relationships, coming from dead, paralysing and sadistic internal objects, to which more recent Kleinian accounts would give more emphasis. Unshiftable, destructive forces, lodged deeply in the personality, are seen to belong, in the author's view to Klein's ‘more pessimistic notions about human nature’, near the end of her life (in 1958) (p. 89).
